annotations.label: Pole of a complex function

anchor.snippet: a point z 0 is a pole of a function f if it is a zero of the function 1/ f and 1/ f is holomorphic

annotations.note: Mathlib has no standalone `Pole` predicate, but poles are captured by `meromorphicOrderAt f x < 0` together with `MeromorphicAt.inv` characterising 1/f locally.

annotations.label: Meromorphic function

anchor.snippet: A function f is meromorphic in an open set U if for every point z of U there is a neighborhood of z in which at least one of f and 1/ f is holomorphic.

annotations.note: `MeromorphicOn f U := ∀ x ∈ U, MeromorphicAt f x` uses the (equivalent) local-zpow-times-analytic formulation rather than the 'either f or 1/f is holomorphic' phrasing.

annotations.label: Duality between zeros and poles

anchor.snippet: If f is meromorphic in U , then a zero of f is a pole of 1/ f , and a pole of f is a zero of 1/ f .

annotations.note: Inverting preserves meromorphy via `MeromorphicAt.inv_iff`, and the sign-flip of orders under inversion (`meromorphicOrderAt_inv`) realises the zero↔pole duality.

annotations.label: Sum of multiplicities on extended plane

anchor.snippet: if a function is meromorphic on the whole complex plane plus the point at infinity , then the sum of the multiplicities of its poles equals the sum of the multiplicities of its zeros

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalisation of meromorphy on the Riemann sphere nor the equal-sum-of-orders statement on a compact complex space.

annotations.label: Holomorphic function

anchor.snippet: A function of a complex variable z is holomorphic in an open domain U if it is differentiable with respect to z at every point of U .

annotations.note: Holomorphy on an open set is expressed via `DifferentiableOn ℂ` / `AnalyticOnNhd ℂ`, which coincide for complex-differentiable functions.

annotations.label: Meromorphic function (Definitions)

anchor.snippet: A function is meromorphic in U if every point of U has a neighbourhood such that at least one of f and 1/ f is holomorphic in it.

annotations.note: Same notion as the lead, formalised by `MeromorphicOn` using the equivalent zpow-smul-analytic local form.

annotations.label: Zero and pole of a meromorphic function

anchor.snippet: A zero of a meromorphic function f is a complex number z such that f ( z ) = 0 . A pole of f is a zero of 1/ f .

annotations.note: Zero/pole sets are encoded indirectly via the integer-valued `divisor` (positive ↔ zero, negative ↔ pole); no named `Zero`/`Pole` predicates exist.

annotations.label: Order of a pole and zero

anchor.snippet: If n > 0 , then

annotations.note: `meromorphicOrderAt f x : WithTop ℤ` is precisely the signed order, with `meromorphicOrderAt_eq_int_iff` giving the local `(z - x)^n • g` characterisation.

annotations.label: Zeros and poles are isolated

anchor.snippet: This characterization of zeros and poles implies that zeros and poles are isolated , that is, every zero or pole has a neighbourhood that does not contain any other zero and pole.

annotations.note: The isolated-zero principle and `MeromorphicOn.codiscreteWithin_setOf_ne_zero` in `IsolatedZeros.lean` show the zero/pole set is discrete.

annotations.label: Sign convention for orders

anchor.snippet: it is often useful to consider a pole of order n as a zero of order − n and a zero of order n as a pole of order − n

annotations.note: Mathlib's order takes values in `WithTop ℤ`, so poles correspond to negative integers by definition, matching the sign convention.

annotations.label: Gamma and zeta as meromorphic examples

anchor.snippet: This is the case for the gamma function (see the image in the infobox), which is meromorphic in the whole complex plane, and has a simple pole at every non-positive integer.

annotations.note: `Complex.Gamma` exists and is shown differentiable away from non-positive integers, but a `MeromorphicOn Complex.Gamma Set.univ` statement and the pole-order computation are not present.

annotations.label: Laurent series characterization

anchor.snippet: a nonzero meromorphic function f is the sum of a Laurent series with at most finite principal part

annotations.note: The local representation `f z = (z - x)^n • g z` with `g` analytic is formalised, but no explicit Laurent-series-with-finite-principal-part object is constructed.

annotations.label: Meromorphic at infinity

anchor.snippet: is meromorphic at infinity if it is meromorphic in some neighbourhood of infinity (that is outside some disk ), and there is an integer n such that

annotations.note: Mathlib's meromorphy is defined only at points of `𝕜`; there is no `MeromorphicAt ∞` notion via the one-point compactification.

annotations.label: Pole/zero at infinity

anchor.snippet: the point at infinity is a pole of order n if n > 0 , and a zero of order

annotations.note: No order at infinity is defined for meromorphic functions in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Polynomial pole at infinity

anchor.snippet: a polynomial of degree n has a pole of degree n at infinity

annotations.note: Mathlib does not state that a polynomial has a pole of order equal to its degree at the point at infinity.

annotations.label: Riemann sphere

anchor.snippet: The complex plane extended by a point at infinity is called the Riemann sphere .

annotations.note: The underlying one-point compactification `OnePoint ℂ` exists topologically, but Mathlib has no dedicated `RiemannSphere` term with its complex-manifold structure.

annotations.label: Sum of orders on Riemann sphere

anchor.snippet: If f is a function that is meromorphic on the whole Riemann sphere, then it has a finite number of zeros and poles, and the sum of the orders of its poles equals the sum of the orders of its zeros.

annotations.note: No statement about meromorphy on the Riemann sphere or a vanishing degree of its divisor is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rational functions on Riemann sphere

anchor.snippet: Every rational function is meromorphic on the whole Riemann sphere, and, in this case, the sum of orders of the zeros or of the poles is the maximum of the degrees of the numerator and the denominator.

annotations.note: Mathlib formalises rational functions algebraically but does not prove their meromorphy on the Riemann sphere or the degree formula for the sum of orders.

annotations.label: Simple pole and zero at infinity

anchor.snippet: is meromorphic on the whole Riemann sphere. It has a pole of order 1 or simple pole at

annotations.note: This worked example, including the behavior at infinity, is not formalised.

annotations.label: Poles of order 2 and 3 with quadruple zero

anchor.snippet: It has a pole of order 2 at

annotations.note: Specific example with prescribed pole and zero orders is not formalised.

annotations.label: Meromorphic on plane but not at infinity

anchor.snippet: is meromorphic in the whole complex plane, but not at infinity. It has poles of order 1 at

annotations.note: This particular essential-singularity-at-infinity example is not formalised.

annotations.label: Single pole at infinity and zero at origin

anchor.snippet: has a single pole at infinity of order 1, and a single zero at the origin.

annotations.note: Identifying f(z)=z as having a pole at infinity and a zero at 0 on the Riemann sphere is not formalised.

annotations.label: Zeros and poles on a complex curve

anchor.snippet: The concept of zeros and poles extends naturally to functions on a complex curve , that is complex analytic manifold of dimension one (over the complex numbers).

annotations.note: Mathlib has no API defining meromorphic functions or their zeros/poles on a general one-dimensional complex manifold.

annotations.label: Holomorphic/meromorphic on a curve via charts

anchor.snippet: This function is holomorphic (resp. meromorphic) in a neighbourhood of a point z of M if there is a chart

annotations.note: The chart-based definition of (mero)morphy on a complex manifold is not present in Mathlib's meromorphic API.

annotations.label: Sum of orders on compact curve

anchor.snippet: If the curve is compact , and the function f is meromorphic on the whole curve, then the number of zeros and poles is finite, and the sum of the orders of the poles equals the sum of the orders of the zeros.

annotations.note: The theorem that a meromorphic function on a compact complex curve has divisor of degree zero is not formalised in Mathlib.
